flashcards.q: What is climate?
flashcards.a: Sum total of weather conditions and variations over a large area for more than thirty years.

flashcards.q: What is weather?
flashcards.a: State of the atmosphere over an area at any point of time; short-term and changeable.

flashcards.q: Define monsoon.
flashcards.a: Seasonal reversal in wind direction during a year. Derived from Arabic word 'mausim' meaning season.

flashcards.q: What type of climate does India have?
flashcards.a: Monsoon type of climate.

flashcards.q: What is the Tropic of Cancer?
flashcards.a: Imaginary line at 23.5°N passing through Rann of Kuchchh to Mizoram, dividing India into tropical (south) and subtropical (north) zones.

flashcards.q: Which mountains protect India from cold Central Asian winds?
flashcards.a: The Himalayas (average height ~6,000m).

flashcards.q: What is the highest rainfall region in India?
flashcards.a: Mawsynram in Khasi Hills, Meghalaya (~11,873 mm annually—highest in the world).

flashcards.q: What is the lowest rainfall region in India?
flashcards.a: Ladakh and western Rajasthan (<10 cm annually).

flashcards.q: Which winds blow during winter in India?
flashcards.a: North-easterly trade winds (dry, originating from subtropical high-pressure belt).

flashcards.q: Which winds blow during summer in India?
flashcards.a: South-westerly monsoon winds (moisture-laden, originating from warm ocean areas).

flashcards.q: What causes the seasonal reversal of winds in India?
flashcards.a: Shift in global heat belt northward in summer and southward in winter, creating pressure differences that reverse wind direction.

flashcards.q: Name the four main seasons of India.
flashcards.a: Cold Weather (Nov-Feb), Hot Weather (Mar-May), Advancing Monsoon (Jun-Sep), Retreating Monsoon (Oct-Nov).

flashcards.q: What is the temperature range in Delhi?
flashcards.a: Winter: 5-15°C; Summer: 40-45°C. Extreme seasonal variation due to continentality.

flashcards.q: What is the temperature range in Mumbai?
flashcards.a: Winter: 20-25°C; Summer: 25-30°C. Equable climate due to sea moderation.

flashcards.q: What is diurnal temperature range in the Thar Desert?
flashcards.a: Day: 50°C; Night: 15°C. Range of ~35°C due to low humidity and lack of cloud cover.

flashcards.q: What is diurnal temperature range in coastal areas like Kerala?
flashcards.a: Minimal (~2-5°C) due to moderating influence of the sea.

flashcards.q: What is continentality?
flashcards.a: Extreme weather conditions (very hot summers, very cold winters) experienced far from the sea due to lack of moderating influence.

flashcards.q: What is the rain shadow effect?
flashcards.a: Phenomenon where leeward side of mountains receives less rainfall as moist air loses moisture on windward side.

flashcards.q: Which coast of India receives winter rainfall (Oct-Nov)?
flashcards.a: Tamil Nadu coast (Coromandel Coast) due to cyclonic depressions from Andaman Sea.

flashcards.q: What are 'mahawat' rains?
flashcards.a: Winter rainfall in northern India brought by cyclonic disturbances from Mediterranean region; crucial for rabi crops.

flashcards.q: What is the 'loo'?
flashcards.a: Strong, hot, dry winds blowing during day in March-May over north and northwestern India; can be fatal.

flashcards.q: What are 'mango showers'?
flashcards.a: Pre-monsoon showers in Kerala and Karnataka helping mangoes ripen early, occurring towards end of summer season.

flashcards.q: What is 'Kaal Baisakhi'?
flashcards.a: Severe thunderstorms with violent winds and hail occurring in West Bengal during hot weather season.

flashcards.q: What is 'October heat'?
flashcards.a: Oppressive weather during Oct-Nov transition season due to high temperature and humidity while monsoon retreats.

flashcards.q: Define 'breaks' in monsoon.
flashcards.a: Alternating wet and dry spells during monsoon season due to movement of monsoon trough; period of little/no rain interspersed with heavy rains.

flashcards.q: What is the monsoon trough?
flashcards.a: Elongated low-pressure area over Indian subcontinent during monsoon; its position determines rainfall distribution across regions.

flashcards.q: What is the Coriolis force?
flashcards.a: Apparent force from earth's rotation deflecting winds right in northern hemisphere and left in southern hemisphere; explains south-westerly direction of monsoon winds.

flashcards.q: Which regions receive over 400 cm annual rainfall?
flashcards.a: Western coast and northeastern India (Malabar coast, Assam, Meghalaya).

flashcards.q: Which regions receive less than 60 cm annual rainfall?
flashcards.a: Western Rajasthan, interior Deccan Plateau, Ladakh, and leeward side of Western Ghats.

flashcards.q: What percentage of India's rainfall comes from south-west monsoon?
flashcards.a: Approximately 80% of India's annual rainfall comes from the south-west monsoon (Jun-Sep).

flashcards.q: When does the monsoon typically arrive in India?
flashcards.a: By early June; it takes about a month to cover the entire country except the extreme northwest.

flashcards.q: When does the monsoon withdraw from northern plains?
flashcards.a: By the beginning of October as the low-pressure system weakens and the sun moves southward.

flashcards.q: What is the average velocity of monsoon winds?
flashcards.a: About 30 km per hour.

flashcards.q: What is 'kharif' crop?
flashcards.a: Summer/monsoon crops (Jun-Oct) requiring heavy rainfall; includes rice, cotton, sugarcane, pulses.

flashcards.q: What is 'rabi' crop?
flashcards.a: Winter crops (Oct-Mar) sown after monsoon; depend on winter rainfall and stored moisture; includes wheat, gram, mustard.

flashcards.q: Which deltas are frequently struck by tropical cyclones?
flashcards.a: Godavari, Krishna, and Kaveri deltas on the eastern coast.

flashcards.q: Why are areas of high rainfall flood-prone?
flashcards.a: Excessive moisture from intense monsoon activity; concentrated rainfall can exceed drainage system capacity.

flashcards.q: Why are areas of low rainfall drought-prone?
flashcards.a: Entire dependence on monsoon success; any shortage causes immediate water stress with no reserves.

flashcards.q: What is a tropical cyclone?
flashcards.a: Severe rotating storm originating over warm ocean waters; brings heavy rainfall and destructive winds when crossing coastal areas.

flashcards.q: Where do tropical cyclones originate that affect India?
flashcards.a: Andaman Sea and Bay of Bengal, moving westward toward eastern Indian coasts.

flashcards.q: What is orographic rainfall?
flashcards.a: Rainfall caused by moist air being forced to rise over high mountains, cooling and precipitating moisture; causes heavy rain on windward slopes.

flashcards.q: Which side of Western Ghats receives heavy rainfall?
flashcards.a: Windward side (western facing Arabian Sea) receives 250+ cm due to orographic effect.

flashcards.q: Which side of Western Ghats remains dry?
flashcards.a: Leeward side (eastern facing Deccan Plateau) remains relatively dry due to rain shadow effect.

flashcards.q: What is the highest temperature recorded in India?
flashcards.a: Around 50°C in parts of Rajasthan desert during summer.

flashcards.q: What is the lowest temperature recorded in India?
flashcards.a: Around -45°C in Drass, Jammu and Kashmir during winter.

flashcards.q: How does the sea moderate coastal climate?
flashcards.a: Sea absorbs heat in summer and releases it in winter, keeping coastal areas warmer in winter and cooler in summer; reduces temperature fluctuations.

flashcards.q: How do ocean currents affect Indian coastal climate?
flashcards.a: Warm currents increase coastal temperatures; cold currents decrease them, especially when combined with onshore winds.

flashcards.q: What role do relief features play in determining climate?
flashcards.a: High mountains act as barriers for winds and cause precipitation if in monsoon path; leeward areas remain dry.

flashcards.q: What is the significance of the monsoon for Indian agriculture?
flashcards.a: Determines agricultural calendar; 80% of rainfall comes from monsoon; success/failure determines food security and farm income.

flashcards.q: Why is the monsoon called a 'unifying bond' for India?
flashcards.a: Seasonal monsoon rhythm connects all regions through shared agricultural calendar, water systems, and cultural practices despite geographic diversity.

flashcards.q: What are the six climatic controls affecting any region?
flashcards.a: Latitude, altitude, pressure and wind systems, continentality (distance from sea), ocean currents, and relief features.

flashcards.q: How does latitude affect temperature?
flashcards.a: Due to earth's curvature, solar radiation varies by latitude; temperature generally decreases from equator toward poles.

flashcards.q: How does altitude affect temperature?
flashcards.a: As altitude increases, atmosphere becomes less dense and temperature decreases (roughly 0.6°C per 100m elevation gain).

flashcards.q: What is the average annual rainfall in Delhi?
flashcards.a: About 60 cm, mostly concentrated in Jun-Sep monsoon months.

flashcards.q: What is the average annual rainfall in Mumbai?
flashcards.a: About 180 cm, with significant rainfall during monsoon and lower amounts other seasons.

flashcards.q: Which is the coldest month in northern India?
flashcards.a: January (average ~5-10°C in northern plains).

flashcards.q: Which is the hottest month in India?
flashcards.a: May (temperatures reach 45°C+ in northwestern regions).

flashcards.q: What climatic characteristics define the cold weather season?
flashcards.a: Clear skies, low temperatures, low humidity, feeble winds, dry conditions (except Tamil Nadu coast).

flashcards.q: What climatic characteristics define the hot weather season?
flashcards.a: Rising temperatures, falling pressure, dust storms, 'loo' winds, thunderstorms with violent winds and hail.

flashcards.q: What climatic characteristics define the advancing monsoon season?
flashcards.a: Heavy rainfall, weather reversal, cloudy skies, high humidity, strong winds, alternating wet and dry spells.

flashcards.q: What climatic characteristics define the retreating monsoon season?
flashcards.a: Clear skies, rising temperatures, high daytime heat, cool nights, high humidity ('October heat'), cyclonic activity on east coast.

flashcards.q: Why does rainfall decrease from east to west in northern plains?
flashcards.a: Monsoon winds lose moisture as they travel inland away from Bay of Bengal; by the time they reach western regions, moisture is depleted.

flashcards.q: Which wind pattern dominates India in winter?
flashcards.a: High-pressure system north of Himalayas with light, variable winds from west-northwest through Ganga valley.

flashcards.q: Which wind pattern dominates India in summer?
flashcards.a: Low-pressure area over interior Asia and northwestern India, with outflow of monsoon trough and south-westerly winds at surface.

flashcards.q: What is the significance of Mawsynram?
flashcards.a: Receives world's highest average rainfall (~11,873 mm); famous for stalagmite and stalactite caves; lies in Khasi Hills.

flashcards.q: Why doesn't the monsoon cover the entire country in the first month?
flashcards.a: Monsoon advances northward gradually over a month; reaches most of country by early July except extreme northwest.

flashcards.q: What causes the monsoon to be 'irregular' in arrival?
flashcards.a: Depends on specific pressure patterns and temperature gradients that don't always materialize on schedule; can arrive early or delayed.

flashcards.q: How do thick walls in Rajasthan houses reflect climate adaptation?
flashcards.a: Provide thermal mass and insulation against extreme heat; reduce internal temperature fluctuations in harsh desert climate.

flashcards.q: Why are houses in Goa/Mangalore built with sloping roofs?
flashcards.a: Allow quick drainage of heavy monsoon rainfall; prevent water accumulation and seepage in high-rainfall coastal regions.

flashcards.q: Why are Assam houses built on stilts?
flashcards.a: Protect from frequent flooding caused by heavy monsoon rainfall and river overflow in flood-prone regions.

flashcards.q: What is the relationship between variability and vulnerability?
flashcards.a: Variability is highest in low-rainfall regions, making them most vulnerable to drought; high-rainfall regions vulnerable to floods.

flashcards.q: What does 'uncertain monsoon' mean for farming?
flashcards.a: Cannot guarantee production; delayed arrival disrupts planting; breaks cause crop failure; uneven distribution creates regional inequality.

flashcards.q: Which five elements describe weather and climate?
flashcards.a: Temperature, atmospheric pressure, wind, humidity, and precipitation.
